edges.description: Loss of ETF system activity blocks electron flow into the CoQ pool.
edges.target: Impaired electron transfer from FAD-dependent dehydrogenases to ubiquinone

edges.description: ETFDH-mutant neuronal cell models show excessive apoptosis and neurite outgrowth defects through mitochondrial apoptosis signaling.
edges.target: Oxidative stress and apoptosis in neuronal cells

edges.description: ETFDH deficiency disrupts the ETFDH-CIII-COQ2 organization that routes lipid-derived electrons into the respiratory chain.
edges.source: Impaired electron transfer from FAD-dependent dehydrogenases to ubiquinone
edges.target: ETFDH-driven metabolon disruption and OXPHOS dysfunction

edges.description: Impaired mitochondrial fuel oxidation limits fasting energy production and ketogenesis, producing acute metabolic crises.
edges.source: Impaired electron transfer from FAD-dependent dehydrogenases to ubiquinone

edges.description: Blocked ETF/ETFDH-dependent oxidation produces the broad acylcarnitine abnormalities used to diagnose MADD.
edges.source: Impaired electron transfer from FAD-dependent dehydrogenases to ubiquinone

edges.description: Impaired ETF/ETF:QO electron transfer produces the glutaric aciduria biochemical hallmark of MADD.
edges.source: Impaired electron transfer from FAD-dependent dehydrogenases to ubiquinone

edges.description: Impaired fatty acid oxidation in skeletal muscle causes lipid storage myopathy.
edges.source: Impaired electron transfer from FAD-dependent dehydrogenases to ubiquinone

edges.description: Accumulated organic acids during decompensation produce severe metabolic acidosis.
Impaired fatty acid oxidation limits hepatic ketogenesis and fasting energy production, causing hypoketotic hypoglycemia.
Severe decompensation can include hyperammonemia, which contributes to acute neurologic deterioration.
Acute energy failure and hyperammonemia can progress to encephalopathy during metabolic crisis.
Mitochondrial fuel oxidation failure can affect myocardium and contribute to cardiomyopathy in severe MADD.
Severe metabolic and neuromuscular involvement can include respiratory dysfunction requiring ventilatory support.
Metabolic decompensation may include recurrent or cyclical vomiting before overt neuromuscular presentation.
Severe infantile MADD can involve hepatic enlargement during systemic metabolic decompensation.
Severe infantile energy failure can manifest as marked muscular hypotonia.
Reduced FAD synthesis limits FAD-dependent dehydrogenase function and phenocopies the core MADD electron-transfer defect.
edges.target: Impaired electron transfer from FAD-dependent dehydrogenases to ubiquinone

edges.description: FLAD1-RRMADD and ETFDH-RRMADD both show lipid storage myopathy in muscle.
Mitochondrial stress in riboflavin-responsive MADD is reflected by increased GDF-15 expression in muscle and serum.
edges.source: ETFDH-driven metabolon disruption and OXPHOS dysfunction

edges.description: Neurite outgrowth defects, axonal degeneration, and neuronal apoptosis provide a cellular mechanism for peripheral neuropathy in the MADD spectrum.
edges.source: Oxidative stress and apoptosis in neuronal cells

edges.description: Sertraline-associated cases showed lipid storage myopathy on muscle biopsy.
Sertraline-associated acquired MADD is marked by elevated acylcarnitines.
Sertraline-associated acquired MADD cases presented with elevated CK.
Lipid storage myopathy injures skeletal muscle and produces the proximal limb weakness typical of late-onset MADD.
Myopathic injury causes elevated serum creatine kinase.
Skeletal muscle injury is reflected biochemically by increased serum creatine kinase.
Skeletal muscle energy failure and lipid storage myopathy reduce exercise capacity.
Skeletal muscle lipid storage and energy failure can manifest as muscle pain in late-onset MADD.
Episodic skeletal muscle energy failure and myopathic injury can manifest as rhabdomyolysis.

edges.target: Impaired electron transfer from FAD-dependent dehydrogenases to ubiquinone
Oxidative stress and apoptosis in neuronal cells

edges.description: Broad acylcarnitine elevations report the combined FAD-dependent dehydrogenase block caused by impaired ETF/ETFDH electron transfer.
edges.source: Impaired electron transfer from FAD-dependent dehydrogenases to ubiquinone

edges.description: Glutaric acid accumulation is a urine organic-acid readout of secondary FAD-dependent dehydrogenase dysfunction in MADD.
edges.source: Impaired electron transfer from FAD-dependent dehydrogenases to ubiquinone

edges.description: Increased serum CK tracks skeletal muscle injury in MADD lipid storage myopathy and can normalize with treatment response.
Increased GDF-15 reports mitochondrial stress in riboflavin-responsive MADD.
edges.source: ETFDH-driven metabolon disruption and OXPHOS dysfunction

edges.description: Abnormal free carnitine together with elevated acylcarnitine/carnitine ratios reports acyl-CoA handling imbalance downstream of the MADD dehydrogenase block.
edges.source: Impaired electron transfer from FAD-dependent dehydrogenases to ubiquinone

nodes.description: Emergency management during metabolic crises includes IV dextrose infusion to suppress catabolism, correction of metabolic acidosis, and initiation of riboflavin therapy. Mechanical ventilation may be required for respiratory failure.

evidence.reference_title: Late-onset multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency: an insidious presentation.
evidence.snippet: This report describes a woman in her 30s who presented with acute-onset ataxia, confusion and hyperammonaemic encephalopathy requiring intubation.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Agents/circumstances to avoid: Inadequate caloric provision during stressors (including following vaccination); prolonged fasting; dehydration; high-fat, high-protein diet; volatile anesthetics and those that contain high doses of long-chain fatty acids; administration of intravenous intralipids during an acute metabolic crisis.

evidence.reference_title: Deep Intronic ETFDH Variants Represent a Recurrent Pathogenic Event in Multiple Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Clinical diagnosis is supported by urinary organic acid and blood acylcarnitine analysis using tandem mass spectrometry in newborn screening programs.

evidence.reference_title: ETFDH mutations as a major cause of riboflavin-responsive multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenation deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Urine organic acid and plasma acyl-carnitine profiles indicated MADD.

readouts.interpretation: Broad acylcarnitine elevations report the combined FAD-dependent dehydrogenase block caused by impaired ETF/ETFDH electron transfer.
readouts.target: Impaired electron transfer from FAD-dependent dehydrogenases to ubiquinone

nodes.description: Cardiac involvement including cardiomyopathy may occur in severe or early-onset forms and can also develop during metabolic crises.

evidence.reference_title: Late-onset multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency: an insidious presentation.
evidence.snippet: The clinical manifestations of MADD are highly variable and include exercise intolerance, myopathy, cardiomyopathy, encephalopathy, coma and death.

nodes.description: CoQ10 supplementation is used as adjunctive therapy in MADD. In cellular models, CoQ10 mitigates apoptosis and neurite outgrowth defects caused by ETFDH mutations.

evidence.reference_title: ETFDH mutation involves excessive apoptosis and neurite outgrowth defect via Bcl2 pathway.
evidence.snippet: Subsequent treatment of the mutant cells with coenzyme Q10 downregulated activated protein expression and mitigated neurite growth defects.

evidence.reference_title: 2-[(18)F] FDG PET/CT in Rapid Late-Onset Multiple Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency: A Case Report.
evidence.snippet: After 1 month of dietary intervention and daily diet supplements (riboflavin 400 mg TID, levocarnitine 1 g TID, Q10 150 mg qD in two doses), the patient had almost recovered to his habitual level.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency: Phenotypic and Genetic Features of a Malaysian Cohort.
evidence.snippet: All 14 demonstrated proximal limb weakness, elevated serum creatine kinase levels, and myopathic changes in electromyography.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Deficiency Is Associated with Sertraline Use - Is There an Acquired Form?
evidence.snippet: All presented with muscle weakness and elevated levels of creatine kinase.

readouts.interpretation: Increased serum CK tracks skeletal muscle injury in MADD lipid storage myopathy and can normalize with treatment response.

nodes.description: Deep intronic variants in ETFDH intron 1 causing pseudo-exon inclusion have been identified as a recurrent pathogenic mechanism that is missed by standard exome sequencing.

evidence.reference_title: Deep Intronic ETFDH Variants Represent a Recurrent Pathogenic Event in Multiple Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
Here, we report a unique
deep intronic mutation in intron 1 of the ETFDH gene, c.35-959A>G, in a patient
with early-onset lethal MADD, resulting in pseudo-exon inclusion.

nodes.description: Dietary strategies focus on avoiding prolonged fasting and limiting fat/protein intake while ensuring adequate carbohydrate supply. During acute crises, IV dextrose is used to suppress catabolism.

evidence.reference_title: Late-onset multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency: an insidious presentation.
evidence.snippet: MADD is treatable with riboflavin and appropriate nutrition with a focus on prevention and early management of metabolic decompensation.

evidence.reference_title: 2-[(18)F] FDG PET/CT in Rapid Late-Onset Multiple Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency: A Case Report.
evidence.snippet: After 1 month of dietary intervention and daily diet supplements (riboflavin 400 mg TID, levocarnitine 1 g TID, Q10 150 mg qD in two doses), the patient had almost recovered to his habitual level.

nodes.description: Biallelic pathogenic variants in ETFA, ETFB, or ETFDH reduce catalytic electron-transfer capacity of the ETF system.

evidence.reference_title: ETFDH mutations as a major cause of riboflavin-responsive multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenation deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenation deficiency (MADD) is a disorder of fatty acid, amino acid and choline metabolism that can result from defects in two flavoproteins, electron transfer flavoprotein (ETF) or ETF: ubiquinone oxidoreductase (ETF:QO).

evidence.reference_title: ETFA / multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: ETFA | HGNC:3481 | multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency | MONDO:0009282 | AR | Definitive

evidence.reference_title: Deep Intronic ETFDH Variants Represent a Recurrent Pathogenic Event in Multiple Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
MADD is an autosomal recessive trait caused by biallelic mutations in
the ETFA, ETFB, and ETFDH genes encoding the alpha and beta subunits of the
electron transfer flavoprotein (ETF) and ETF-coenzyme Q oxidoreductase enzymes.

evidence.reference_title: ETFB / multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: ETFB | HGNC:3482 | multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency | MONDO:0009282 | AR | Definitive

evidence.reference_title: Deep Intronic ETFDH Variants Represent a Recurrent Pathogenic Event in Multiple Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet:
MADD is an autosomal recessive trait caused by biallelic mutations in
the ETFA, ETFB, and ETFDH genes encoding the alpha and beta subunits of the
electron transfer flavoprotein (ETF) and ETF-coenzyme Q oxidoreductase enzymes.

evidence.reference_title: ETFDH / multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency (Definitive)
evidence.snippet: ETFDH | HGNC:3483 | multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency | MONDO:0009282 | AR | Definitive

nodes.description: Most common ETFDH variant in southern Chinese and East Asian populations. Found in 86% of patients in the Malaysian MADD cohort.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency: Phenotypic and Genetic Features of a Malaysian Cohort.
evidence.snippet: Notably, 12 (86%) patients, including the 2 Malay sisters, carried a common c.250G>A (p.A84T) variant, consistent with the hotspot mutation reported in southern China.

evidence.reference_title: ETFDH mutations as a major cause of riboflavin-responsive multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenation deficiency.
evidence.snippet: All patients had mutations in the gene for ETF:QO.

nodes.description: Recent work demonstrates that ETFDH participates in a metabolon comprising ETFDH, respiratory chain complex III (CIII), and the CoQ biosynthesis regulator COQ2. This complex maintains coenzyme Q homeostasis, minimizes reduced-Q reductive stress, and supports OXPHOS efficiency. Loss of ETFDH causes CIII dysfunction, pathological QH2 accumulation, reductive stress, and increased ROS.
nodes.id: ETFDH-driven metabolon disruption and OXPHOS dysfunction

evidence.reference_title: An ETFDH-driven metabolon supports OXPHOS efficiency in skeletal muscle by regulating coenzyme Q homeostasis.
evidence.snippet: We identify a complex (comprising ETFDH, CIII and the Q-biosynthesis regulator COQ2) that directs electrons from lipid substrates to the respiratory chain, thereby reducing electron leaks and reactive oxygen species production.

nodes.description: Serum creatine kinase is elevated in virtually all MADD patients, reflecting ongoing skeletal muscle damage.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency: Phenotypic and Genetic Features of a Malaysian Cohort.
evidence.snippet: All 14 demonstrated proximal limb weakness, elevated serum creatine kinase levels, and myopathic changes in electromyography.

nodes.description: Acute encephalopathy with confusion and altered consciousness can occur during metabolic crises, sometimes accompanied by hyperammonemia.

evidence.reference_title: ETFDH mutations as a major cause of riboflavin-responsive multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenation deficiency.
evidence.snippet: All the index cases presented with encephalopathy or muscle weakness or a combination of these symptoms

evidence.reference_title: Late-onset multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency: an insidious presentation.
evidence.snippet: This report describes a woman in her 30s who presented with acute-onset ataxia, confusion and hyperammonaemic encephalopathy requiring intubation.

nodes.description: Exercise intolerance is a common symptom in late-onset MADD, reflecting impaired energy metabolism in skeletal muscle.

evidence.reference_title: Late-onset multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency: an insidious presentation.
evidence.snippet: The clinical manifestations of MADD are highly variable and include exercise intolerance, myopathy, cardiomyopathy, encephalopathy, coma and death.

evidence.reference_title: A comparative study on riboflavin responsive multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenation deficiency due to variants in FLAD1 and ETFDH gene.
evidence.snippet: Our data revealed that FLAD1-RRMADD (p.Arg530) has similar clinical, biochemical, and fatty acid metabolism changes to ETFDH-RRMADD except for muscle pathological features.

nodes.description: FLAD1 variants impair flavin adenine dinucleotide synthesis and can produce a riboflavin-responsive MADD-like lipid storage myopathy with biochemical and fatty-acid metabolism changes overlapping ETFDH-related MADD.

evidence.reference_title: A comparative study on riboflavin responsive multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenation deficiency due to variants in FLAD1 and ETFDH gene.
evidence.snippet: We described here a case with riboflavin responsive LSM and MADD resulting from FLAD1 gene variants

evidence.reference_title: Urinary excretion of l-carnitine and acylcarnitines by patients with disorders of organic acid metabolism: evidence for secondary insufficiency of l-carnitine.
evidence.snippet: In all cases, concentrations of acylcarnitines were greatly increased above normal with free carnitine concentrations ranging from undetectable to supranormal values.

evidence.reference_title: Clinical, biochemical, and genetic spectrum of MADD in a South African cohort: an ICGNMD study.
evidence.snippet: urinary metabolic markers for MADD improved/normalised following treatment with riboflavin and L-carnitine.

readouts.interpretation: Abnormal free carnitine together with elevated acylcarnitine/carnitine ratios reports acyl-CoA handling imbalance downstream of the MADD dehydrogenase block.

evidence.reference_title: A comparative study on riboflavin responsive multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenation deficiency due to variants in FLAD1 and ETFDH gene.
evidence.snippet: Molecular study revealed that the expression of GDF15 gene in muscle and GDF15 protein in both serum and muscle was significantly increased in FLAD1-RRMADD and ETFDH-RRMADD groups.

evidence.reference_title: Very-late-onset multiple Acyl-coenzyme a dehydrogenase deficiency with elevated GDF-15 and Aldolase: a case report.
evidence.snippet: This case adds to the growing literature on the clinical heterogeneity of VLO-MADD, comments on the potential for non-genetic, pharmacologic triggers like sertraline, and highlights that GDF-15 and aldolase can be elevated with normal CK.

readouts.interpretation: Increased GDF-15 reports mitochondrial stress in riboflavin-responsive MADD.
readouts.target: ETFDH-driven metabolon disruption and OXPHOS dysfunction

evidence.snippet: Clinical diagnosis is supported by urinary organic acid and blood acylcarnitine analysis using tandem mass spectrometry in newborn screening programs.

nodes.description: Hepatomegaly and liver dysfunction are features of severe neonatal presentations of MADD.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple acyl-Co A dehydrogenation deficiency (MADD) in a boy with nonketotic hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, muscle hypotonia and cardiomyopathy. Detection of N-isovalerylglutamic acid and its monoamide.
evidence.snippet: He had hepatomegaly and echocardiographically a non-obstructive cardiomyopathy.

nodes.description: Hyperammonemia can occur during metabolic crises, contributing to encephalopathy and requiring urgent management.

evidence.reference_title: Late-onset multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency: an insidious presentation.
evidence.snippet: Early-onset MADD is often associated with a high mortality with significant number of patients presenting with severe metabolic acidosis, non-ketotic hypoglycaemia and/or hyperammonaemic presentations.

nodes.description: Fasting intolerance with non-ketotic or hypoketotic hypoglycemia due to impaired fatty acid oxidation and inability to generate sufficient ketone bodies.

evidence.reference_title: Late-onset multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency: an insidious presentation.
evidence.snippet: Early-onset MADD is often associated with a high mortality with significant number of patients presenting with severe metabolic acidosis, non-ketotic hypoglycaemia and/or hyperammonaemic presentations.

nodes.description: Disrupted electron flow from multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenases through the ETF/ETFDH system to the mitochondrial coenzyme Q pool blocks fatty acid beta-oxidation, branched-chain amino acid catabolism, and choline oxidation, causing accumulation of metabolic intermediates and energy deficit.
nodes.id: Impaired electron transfer from FAD-dependent dehydrogenases to ubiquinone

evidence.reference_title: ETFDH mutations as a major cause of riboflavin-responsive multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenation deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenation deficiency (MADD) is a disorder of fatty acid, amino acid and choline metabolism that can result from defects in two flavoproteins, electron transfer flavoprotein (ETF) or ETF: ubiquinone oxidoreductase (ETF:QO).

nodes.description: L-carnitine supplementation addresses secondary carnitine depletion caused by increased acylcarnitine conjugation. It supports acyl group handling and excretion.

evidence.reference_title: Inborn Errors of Metabolism with Myopathy: Defects of Fatty Acid Oxidation and the Carnitine Shuttle System.
evidence.snippet: Avoidance of fasting and conjugation of toxic metabolites with L-carnitine and glycine are indicated, and a low-fat diet may be helpful.

evidence.reference_title: 2-[(18)F] FDG PET/CT in Rapid Late-Onset Multiple Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency: A Case Report.
evidence.snippet: After 1 month of dietary intervention and daily diet supplements (riboflavin 400 mg TID, levocarnitine 1 g TID, Q10 150 mg qD in two doses), the patient had almost recovered to his habitual level.

nodes.description: Defective fatty acid oxidation leads to pathological lipid droplet accumulation in skeletal muscle fibers, resulting in lipid storage myopathy. Late-onset MADD is the most common lipid storage myopathy.

evidence.reference_title: The male-to-female ratio in late-onset multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency: a systematic review and meta-analysis.
evidence.snippet: Late-onset multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency (MADD) is the most common lipid storage myopathy.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency: Phenotypic and Genetic Features of a Malaysian Cohort.
evidence.snippet: Multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency (MADD) is an inherited disorder of fatty acid oxidation that causes lipid storage myopathy (LSM).

nodes.description: High-anion-gap metabolic acidosis during acute decompensation is a characteristic feature, particularly in neonatal-onset forms.

evidence.reference_title: Late-onset multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency: an insidious presentation.
evidence.snippet: Early-onset MADD is often associated with a high mortality with significant number of patients presenting with severe metabolic acidosis, non-ketotic hypoglycaemia and/or hyperammonaemic presentations.

nodes.description: Hypotonia is a common feature in neonatal forms and may also be present in late-onset MADD.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple acyl-Co A dehydrogenation deficiency (MADD) in a boy with nonketotic hypoglycemia, hepatomegaly, muscle hypotonia and cardiomyopathy. Detection of N-isovalerylglutamic acid and its monoamide.
evidence.snippet: Later on, development was retarded with a severe muscular hypotonia.

nodes.description: Muscle pain is a common late-onset MADD symptom accompanying proximal weakness and exercise intolerance.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: The most common symptoms are muscle weakness, exercise intolerance, and/or muscle pain

nodes.description: MADD is detectable by newborn screening programs via tandem mass spectrometry acylcarnitine profiling. Early detection enables pre-symptomatic treatment and improved outcomes.

evidence.reference_title: Deep Intronic ETFDH Variants Represent a Recurrent Pathogenic Event in Multiple Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Clinical diagnosis is supported by urinary organic acid and blood acylcarnitine analysis using tandem mass spectrometry in newborn screening programs.

nodes.description: ETFDH mutations lead to oxidative stress, activation of the BCL-2 family/MOMP apoptotic signaling pathway, and neurite outgrowth defects. In cellular models, ETFDH mutations cause increased pro-apoptotic markers including BAX, cytochrome c, caspase-9 and caspase-3, linking mitochondrial redox disturbance to neurodegeneration.
nodes.id: Oxidative stress and apoptosis in neuronal cells

evidence.reference_title: ETFDH mutation involves excessive apoptosis and neurite outgrowth defect via Bcl2 pathway.
evidence.snippet: Our cellular models of MADD exhibit neurite growth defects and excessive apoptosis.

nodes.description: Peripheral neuropathy has been reported in both muscular and neuronal contexts of MADD, particularly in late-onset forms.

evidence.reference_title: ETFDH mutation involves excessive apoptosis and neurite outgrowth defect via Bcl2 pathway.
evidence.snippet: Various phenotypes, including episodic weakness or rhabdomyolysis, exercise intolerance, and peripheral neuropathy, have been reported in both muscular and neuronal contexts.

nodes.description: Progressive proximal limb weakness is the hallmark presentation of late-onset MADD. All patients in clinical cohorts demonstrated proximal limb weakness.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency: Phenotypic and Genetic Features of a Malaysian Cohort.
evidence.snippet: All 14 demonstrated proximal limb weakness, elevated serum creatine kinase levels, and myopathic changes in electromyography.

evidence.reference_title: Late-onset multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency: an insidious presentation.
evidence.snippet: The clinical manifestations of MADD are highly variable and include exercise intolerance, myopathy, cardiomyopathy, encephalopathy, coma and death.

nodes.description: Respiratory dysfunction may occur in MADD, particularly during severe metabolic or neuromuscular presentations. Acute hyperammonemic encephalopathy can require intubation during decompensation.

evidence.reference_title: Inborn Errors of Metabolism with Myopathy: Defects of Fatty Acid Oxidation and the Carnitine Shuttle System.
evidence.snippet: Respiratory dysfunction may be present.

evidence.reference_title: Late-onset multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency: an insidious presentation.
evidence.snippet: This report describes a woman in her 30s who presented with acute-onset ataxia, confusion and hyperammonaemic encephalopathy requiring intubation.

nodes.description: Episodic rhabdomyolysis can occur during metabolic crises and may be a presenting feature. In one cohort, a patient died during severe rhabdomyolysis with acute renal failure.

evidence.reference_title: ETFDH mutation involves excessive apoptosis and neurite outgrowth defect via Bcl2 pathway.
evidence.snippet: Various phenotypes, including episodic weakness or rhabdomyolysis, exercise intolerance, and peripheral neuropathy, have been reported in both muscular and neuronal contexts.

nodes.description: Riboflavin (vitamin B2) is the cornerstone treatment for late-onset MADD. It increases FAD availability and may stabilize mutant ETFDH/ETF-QO protein folding. Doses of 100-400 mg/day produce dramatic improvement in most late-onset patients, with symptom resolution and CK normalization.

evidence.reference_title: ETFDH mutations as a major cause of riboflavin-responsive multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenation deficiency.
evidence.snippet: Clinical and biochemical parameters were either totally or partly corrected after riboflavin treatment.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Acyl-CoA Dehydrogenase Deficiency: Phenotypic and Genetic Features of a Malaysian Cohort.
evidence.snippet: All of the patients responded well to riboflavin therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Deficiency Is Associated with Sertraline Use - Is There an Acquired Form?
evidence.snippet: All 9 patients responded to riboflavin treatment with normalization of creatine kinase and muscle biopsy findings, and in 8 patients the clinical symptoms clearly improved.

nodes.description: Recent evidence suggests sertraline may induce an acquired form of MADD in some patients. Discontinuation of sertraline is warranted when MADD develops in the setting of sertraline use, in addition to riboflavin treatment.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Deficiency Is Associated with Sertraline Use - Is There an Acquired Form?
evidence.snippet: Our findings strongly suggest that sertraline may induce an acquired form of MADD in some patients. Importantly, riboflavin treatment seems to be similarly effective as in genetic MADD, but discontinuation of sertraline is reasonably warranted.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Deficiency Is Associated with Sertraline Use - Is There an Acquired Form?
evidence.snippet: Remarkably, all 7 patients without disease-causing mutations were treated with sertraline. In some cases, a deterioration of symptoms closely followed dose increase, and discontinuation resulted in an improved acylcarnitine profile.

nodes.description: Sertraline exposure has been associated with an acquired late-onset MADD-like phenotype in patients without disease-causing MADD mutations, with muscle weakness, elevated creatine kinase, lipid storage myopathy, and elevated acylcarnitines. The mechanism remains emerging, but the clinical pattern links sertraline exposure to a reversible MADD-like metabolic state.

evidence.reference_title: Multiple Acyl-Coenzyme A Dehydrogenase Deficiency Is Associated with Sertraline Use - Is There an Acquired Form?
evidence.snippet: Our findings strongly suggest that sertraline may induce an acquired form of MADD in some patients.

nodes.description: Catabolic stress and impaired mitochondrial fatty acid oxidation produce a systemic energy crisis, particularly in liver, heart, and brain. Reduced ketone generation, impaired fuel availability, and accumulated organic acids manifest as metabolic acidosis, hypoketotic hypoglycemia, hyperammonemia, encephalopathy, and cardiomyopathy in severe presentations.

biological_processes: generation of precursor metabolites and energy

evidence.reference_title: Late-onset multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenase deficiency: an insidious presentation.
evidence.snippet: Early-onset MADD is often associated with a high mortality with significant number of patients presenting with severe metabolic acidosis, non-ketotic hypoglycaemia and/or hyperammonaemic presentations.

nodes.description: Cyclical vomiting can occur in MADD, sometimes as a presenting symptom preceding the full metabolic phenotype.

evidence.reference_title: ETFDH mutations as a major cause of riboflavin-responsive multiple acyl-CoA dehydrogenation deficiency.
evidence.snippet: several had previously suffered cyclical vomiting.
